title: Natural region

infobox.value: (landscape unit) is a basic geographic unit. Usually, it is a [region](/wiki/Region "Region") which is distinguished by its common natural features of [geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography"), [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology"), and [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Area defined by common geography, geology and climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/The_Burren%27s_western_scarp%2C_from_Murrooghkilly_-_geograph.org.uk_-_150827.jpg/250px-The_Burren%27s_western_scarp%2C_from_Murrooghkilly_-_geograph.org.uk_-_150827.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Burren%27s_western_scarp,_from_Murrooghkilly_-_geograph.org.uk_-_150827.jpg)

[The Burren](/wiki/The_Burren "The Burren"), a vast natural region in Ireland; view of the western scarp

A **natural region** (landscape unit) is a basic geographic unit. Usually, it is a [region](/wiki/Region "Region") which is distinguished by its common natural features of [geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography"), [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology"), and [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From the [ecological](/wiki/Ecology "Ecology") point of view, the naturally occurring flora and fauna of the region are likely to be influenced by its geographical and geological factors, such as [soil](/wiki/Soil "Soil") and [water availability](/wiki/Water_resources "Water resources"), in a significant manner. Thus most natural regions are homogeneous [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem"). Human impact can be an important factor in the shaping and destiny of a particular natural region.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Main terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natural_region&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Main terms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Natural_Regions_of_Chile.svg/250px-Natural_Regions_of_Chile.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Natural_Regions_of_Chile.svg "Natural regions of Chile.")

**[Norte Grande](/wiki/Norte_Grande "Norte Grande")**

**[Norte Chico](/wiki/Norte_Chico,_Chile "Norte Chico, Chile")**

**[Zona Central](/wiki/Zona_Central,_Chile "Zona Central, Chile")**

**[Zona Sur](/wiki/Zona_Sur "Zona Sur")**

**[Zona Austral](/wiki/Zona_Austral "Zona Austral")**

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Natural_Regions_of_Chile.svg/250px-Natural_Regions_of_Chile.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Natural_Regions_of_Chile.svg)

Natural regions of Chile.

The concept "natural region" is a large basic geographical unit, like the vast [boreal forest](/wiki/Boreal_forest "Boreal forest") region.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The term may also be used generically, like in [alpine tundra](/wiki/Alpine_tundra "Alpine tundra"), or specifically to refer to a particular place.

The term is particularly useful where there is no corresponding or coterminous official region. [The Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens") of eastern [England](/wiki/England "England"), the [Thai highlands](/wiki/Thai_highlands "Thai highlands"), and the [Pays de Bray](/wiki/Pays_de_Bray "Pays de Bray") in Normandy, are examples of this. Others might include regions with particular geological characteristics, like [badlands](/wiki/Badlands "Badlands"), such as the [Bardenas Reales](/wiki/Bardenas_Reales "Bardenas Reales"), an upland [massif](/wiki/Massif "Massif") of acidic rock, or [The Burren](/wiki/The_Burren "The Burren"), in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland").
